title: Topological similarity of random cell complexes, and applications
speaker: Benjamin Schweinhart
abstract: Although random cell complexes occur throughout the physical sciences, there does not appear to be a standard way to quantify their statistical similarities and differences. I'll introduce the notions of a 'swatch' and a 'cloth', which provide a description of the local topology of cell complexes which is both general (any physical system that may be represented as a regular cell complex is admissible) and complete (any statistical question about the local topology may be answered from the cloth).

proof_stages.label: Materials motivating topological descriptors
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from disordered physical materials whose structure is not well captured by crystallography or standard molecular descriptors, motivating topology as a source of scale-free and local structural statistics.

proof_stages.label: Regular cell complexes and rooted swatches
proof_stages.summary: Regular cell complexes are encoded by dimension-labelled incidence graphs, and a swatch is defined as a rooted graph-distance ball capturing the local topological configuration around a zero-cell.

proof_stages.label: Cloths, swatch metrics, and convergence
proof_stages.summary: The empirical distributions of swatch types over all roots form the cloth, and distances built from largest common subswatches and earth mover's distance yield a topology in which convergence means convergence of all local topological frequencies, related to Benjamini-Schramm graph limits.

proof_stages.label: Curvature-driven embedded-graph model
proof_stages.summary: The computational case study is a degree-three polygonal network in the three-torus evolving by length/curvature minimization, with specified singular topological moves and contrasting initial conditions.

proof_stages.label: Swatch evidence for a universal steady state
proof_stages.summary: Computing cloth distances for the evolving networks shows rapid approach to a candidate steady state across large Voronoi and random-graph initial conditions, insensitivity to the intersection move in the long term, and finite-size effects quantified by subsampling a large test case.

proof_stages.label: Persistent homology for voids and dual graphs
proof_stages.summary: Persistent homology of expanding neighborhoods is proposed as a way to detect voids in embedded material networks, validate cell recovery, visualize representative cycles, and refine interval data into tree-like splitting information useful for dual-graph reconstruction.

proof_stages.label: Knottedness of embedded material graphs
proof_stages.summary: Preliminary knot statistics for shortest cycles suggest curvature flow tends to unknot networks, leading to a proposed global definition in which an embedded graph is unknotted when its complement has the homotopy type of a graph, equivalently supporting a dual network.
